next_steps: Obtain a current authoritative Morgan record or physical-copy evidence before minting a Huth/Morgan copy entity.
Do not substitute a same-edition Gallica copy for Furnivall's exact exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Can Alfred Huth's exact copy of the Petit/Rembolt circa-1517 edition be verified as Morgan Library PML 62412?

next_steps: Retry one exact official ARK per edition from a working network path.
Avoid duplicate same-edition acquisition unless copies provide distinct evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which one best complete official Gallica carrier can be successfully acquired for each of the Petit/Rembolt and Vérard editions?

next_steps: Check for a newly public complete facsimile separately from the mediated order routes.
If authorized and funded, submit item-specific reproduction enquiries without treating requestability as evidence that images already exist or can be supplied.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public target facsimiles be verified for Vitellius C XIII, Tiberius A VII/1, or Stowe 952?

next_steps: Keep physical-copy identity in capture notes until the schema supports a distinct printed-carrier entity.
openQuestions.question: How should the exact U-M physical bound EETS set scanned in 2003 be modeled if the schema gains a physical-print-copy entity type?

next_steps: Consult manuscript, documentary, and recent Lydgate scholarship; retain begun 1426 until completion evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: When was the full Middle English translation completed?

next_steps: Inspect Ff.5.30 directly or obtain current authoritative foliation documentation.
Preserve both reported locator layers in the meantime.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned, identifier-enriched copy of the 2006 University of Michigan OCR/XML encoding of Furnivall and Locock's three-part EETS edition. That edition prints a composite text from Cotton Vitellius C XIII, Cotton Tiberius A VII/1, and Stowe 952, but substitutes Chaucer's ABC through Furnivall's One-Text/CUL Ff.5.30 chain and supplements the acrostic from a Petit/Rembolt print. Vitellius and Stowe reserve blank ABC space, whereas Tiberius loses the ABC locus through missing leaves. Raw, pinned, and May 2026 current XML layers; 862 carrier images plus two scanner charts; original-part scans; the 1973 Kraus reprint; official catalog sources; and jurisdiction- and layer-specific rights determinations remain distinct.

catalogSummary.author: John Lydgate (attributed translator-adapter)
catalogSummary.date: Translation begun in 1426; completion date unknown
catalogSummary.editor: Frederick J. Furnivall (EETS text parts I-II)
catalogSummary.region: England (likely production context; Paris is the patron's stated location, not a proven place of translation)
catalogSummary.title: The Pilgrimage of the Life of Man

catalogingSubject.label: The Pilgrimage of the Life of Man
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The Middle English pilgrimage work commonly attributed to John Lydgate as translator-adapter: the translator's prologue, author's prologue, and pilgrimage text printed on EETS text pages 1-665. The record excludes Locock's introduction, notes, glossary, indexes, publisher notices, omitted front/back matter, and the physical University of Michigan binding; embedded poems and the prose tract remain internal parts of one work.

contentParts.label: Translator's prologue
notes: The two-line Latin manuscript paratext before numbered line 1 is excluded from the 184 verse lines.
contentParts.summary: Lydgate's opening prologue, including the statement that the translation was begun in 1426.

contentParts.label: Author's prologue
contentParts.summary: The translated author's prologue to the pilgrimage allegory.

contentParts.label: Pilgrimage narrative residual
notes: This is a mutually exclusive accounting residual, not the raw contents of XML node 3.3.
The residual has no defensible commensurable verse-group count because XML LG elements conflate literary groups with print-line, prose, and note structures.
contentParts.summary: The pilgrimage narrative after removing every separately described embedded verse unit and the prose-tract remainder.

contentParts.label: Latin poem on the Articles of the Creed
notes: Forty-three twelve-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: An embedded Latin poem on the Articles of the Creed.

contentParts.label: Latin poem on God in Trinity
notes: Thirty-seven twelve-line stanzas; seventeen note-nested L elements are excluded.
contentParts.summary: An embedded Latin poem on God in Trinity.

contentParts.label: Latin hymn to the Virgin Mary
notes: Fourteen twelve-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: An embedded Latin hymn addressed to the Virgin Mary.

contentParts.label: Prose tract concerning the Virgin
notes: The XML uses 727 L elements for this prose remainder; those are print-line chunks, not verse lines.
The p. 456 illumination-space note is absorbed here and is not subtracted a second time.
contentParts.summary: The prose tract or consolation concerning the Virgin, excluding its embedded four-stanza prayer.

contentParts.label: Mary, be our Refuge in Tribulation!
notes: This is the smallest defensible verse scope: four direct eight-line stanza groups.
The direct child rubric bearing the display title is excluded from the 32 verse lines.
contentParts.summary: A four-stanza Middle English prayer embedded inside the otherwise prose tract.

contentParts.label: Chaucer's ABC (La Prière de Nostre Dame)
notes: Vitellius C XIII and Stowe 952 reserve blank space for the ABC; Tiberius A VII/1 loses its locus through missing leaves. None supplies the inserted EETS text.
contentParts.summary: The twenty-three-stanza ABC attributed to Geoffrey Chaucer and inserted from Furnivall's One-Text edition rather than transcribed from a principal Lydgate witness.

contentParts.label: Deguileville name acrostic
notes: Twenty-four eight-line stanzas; twenty-two note-nested L elements are excluded.
contentParts.summary: The bilingual Latin/Middle French twenty-four-stanza acrostic spelling Guillaume de Deguileville's name, with its later portion supplied from the Petit/Rembolt print.

notes: The prologue proves a beginning date, not a completion date; no normalized not_after value is asserted.

extent.description: One mixed verse/prose work. Ten mutually exclusive internal scopes contribute 25,421 verse lines: 184 + 124 + 23,577 + 516 + 444 + 168 + 0 + 32 + 184 + 192. Sections and aggregate verse groups are unknown because the XML DIV/LG structures are editorial encoding containers rather than stable intrinsic section or stanza axes.

formStatements.description: One verse-dominant work containing a substantial prose tract; internal embedded poems do not make it a compilation.
The 727 XML L rows outside the embedded prayer are visually and intellectually prose print-line chunks.
Mutually exclusive verse scopes contributing 25,421 lines.

scope.description: Sustained embedded Latin verse, including the bilingual Deguileville acrostic; brief paratext and rubric material are excluded from verse accounting

languageStatements.label: Middle French
notes: The main intellectual wording is Middle English; edition-only comparative French quotations are explicitly excluded from this work-language facet.

scope.description: Middle French embedded with Latin in the Deguileville name acrostic

notes: The aggregate 25,421 is the corrected result; 25,389 is retained only as superseded audit history and is not a competing extent value.
The ten content parts are mutually exclusive accounting scopes. The pilgrimage residual excludes the six separately modeled embedded verse units and the prose-tract remainder.
Vitellius C XIII and Stowe 952 deliberately reserve ABC space; Tiberius A VII/1 loses the entire ABC locus through a missing-leaf lacuna. These absences must not be flattened into one category.

next_steps: Consult manuscript, documentary, and recent Lydgate scholarship without converting the prologue's begun-in-1426 statement into a completion date.
openQuestions.question: When was the full Middle English translation completed?

next_steps: Review current attribution scholarship and preserve the differing confidence of British Library records until a stronger synthesis is documented.
openQuestions.question: Can the commonly accepted Lydgate translator-adapter attribution be stated more strongly across current manuscript scholarship?

notes: Paris is deliberately not assigned as the work's composition or translation place.
This statement is about the patron's location only.

scope.description: Location of the patron, Thomas Montagu, Earl of Salisbury, when the translation was begun

notes: This is not an original-author attribution.

scope.description: Middle English translation of Deguileville's second-recension French work
Adaptive shaping of the Middle English work, including source substitutions and embedded materials
The embedded ABC / La Prière de Nostre Dame only

notes: Locock's 1904 apparatus is modeled in lineage and excluded from the work's content-part boundary.

scope.description: Editorial presentation of the Middle English text in EETS Extra Series 77 and 83

subjects.label: Pilgrimage of human life, salvation, and judgment
Personification allegory and Christian moral instruction

summaries.value: A Middle English dream-vision pilgrimage through personified moral and spiritual instruction, translated and adaptively reshaped from Guillaume de Deguileville's 1355 second recension. The verse-dominant work follows the pilgrim's encounters on the way toward salvation and includes sustained Latin poems, Marian devotion, a prose consolation with a four-stanza prayer, Chaucer's ABC, and Deguileville's name acrostic.

The work boundary is EETS text pages 1-665. Locock's introduction, notes, glossary, indexes, omitted front and back matter, publisher notices, and the physical U-M binding are transmission or carrier layers, not additional intellectual items. The internal poems and prose tract remain mutually exclusive parts of one mixed work.

The 25,421-line total is 26,346 XML L elements minus 188 note-nested rows, 727 prose-scope rows outside the prayer, and ten separately excluded paratext, rubric, or non-text rows. The embedded prayer contributes four eight-line stanzas at node 3.3.7.6. All content parts have null item_count and zero item-form metrics because the aggregate owns the single mixed item.

work.summary: A Middle English dream-vision pilgrimage through personified moral and spiritual instruction, translated and adaptively reshaped from Guillaume de Deguileville's 1355 second recension. The verse-dominant work follows the pilgrim's encounters on the way toward salvation and includes sustained Latin poems, Marian devotion, a prose consolation with a four-stanza prayer, Chaucer's ABC, and Deguileville's name acrostic.
